[222:08]And that includes, by the way, launching the missiles that kill Russians.

[222:13]That means providing the coordinates and information for Ukraine to destroy Russian warships.

[222:19]I mean, literally short of Americans pulling the trigger and a bullet coming out and shooting

[222:24]Russians, although that, I mean, that's basically what's already happening.

[222:27]We threw everything at them short of that, and it didn't work.

[222:33]And not only is it not working, but it is getting very expensive for the United States.

[222:38]And by expensive, I don't even mean a dollar amount.

[222:41]It's expensive for us in terms of equipment.

[222:44]Russia is out producing us 20 to 1 in terms of artillery shells, ammunition, armor.

[222:52]Russia has industrial capacity.

[222:54]We don't.

[222:56]So, when we're sending Ukraine all of this aid, it's no longer coming off the shelves,

[223:02]it's coming off of the conveyor belts.

[223:04]We're not giving them our old stock, we're giving them the stuff that we're making new.

[223:09]So, it is bankrupting us in terms of the things that we cannot print, which is not debt or

[223:14]money, but the actual equipment.

[223:16]Not only that, but it is destroying Europe.

[223:21]Because Europe is deprived of cheap Russian energy,

[223:24]in particular, Germany has entered into something like a permanent recession.

[223:29]Their industry, which is a major part of their economy,

[223:33]their manufacturing is no longer economical without cheap Russian gas.

[223:37]And that is a big part of what delivered the recent result in Germany's elections,

[223:41]where the AFD got a second place finish,

[223:44]not just because of immigration, but also because of the recession.

[223:48]the CDU got the plurality of the seats. So you've got a situation where it's

[223:55]bankrupting the United States. Russia is also winning the war. Ukraine is running

[224:03]out of manpower. They're running out of fresh bodies to throw out the front lines,

[224:08]and Russia is taking everything. And not only that, but the United States wants to

[224:16]There is, since the war broke out in Ukraine, a major conflagration in the Middle East between

[224:22]Israel and Iran and Iran's proxies.

[224:24]And time is running out on potentially China's invasion of Taiwan.

[224:38]dates as potential timelines when China will believe that it has achieved parity or supremacy

[224:45]over the US military in the Pacific and we'll make a move on Taiwan.

[224:49]So for many reasons, Trump wants to make a deal, but the problem is there's nothing

[224:57]really that we can do other than capitulate.

[225:01]We have run out of options.

[225:07]Europe's economy is in a recession and now they are upset about what is happening.

[225:12]We are trying to pivot into other theaters. Ukraine is running out of men. Russia is advancing

[225:19]and winning and on the brink of breaking Ukraine's front lines. And so what can be done

[225:25]other than to utterly capitulate?

[225:29]And to make a deal on Russia's terms, and Russia's terms are these, Ukraine will never

[225:35]join NATO.

[225:36]There will be no European troops in Ukraine.

[225:39]That means no American troops from NATO.

[225:41]That means no NATO troops from Europe, no troops from European Union countries.

[225:47]That means Russia keeps all the territory they've taken.

[225:50]And probably Ukraine comes under the Russian sphere of influence.

[225:58]Russia will dictate the terms. The United States will have to accept them. And it will

[226:03]be regarded as a strategic defeat. Even though that was always going to be the outcome. And

[226:08]we talked about this earlier in the week. It is a defeat. It will be framed as a defeat.

[226:15]And yet we have fought on and persisted like this, knowing that that's the outcome because

[226:19]our strategic goal was to degrade Russia's military.

[226:24]We knew they would take the land.

[226:25]We knew we weren't getting it back.

[226:27]We knew Ukraine was not joining NATO indefinitely, but we would hurt Russia's military and that

[226:35]would inflict a strategic loss on Russia.

[226:50]this other than the American media who is keeping the faith or and the Europeans and

[226:57]in particular, the United kingdom and Macron. And apparently now the newly elected chancellor

[227:02]in Germany, everybody seems to understand this war is over. It's done.

[227:08]other than the, like the Washington post and these Western European countries. So Trump

[227:13]gets in and says, it's, it's over. I want to make a deal. I don't want to be fighting

[227:17]in Ukraine. He wants to back up Israel. He wants to free up America's military to potentially

[227:26]destroy Iran, but really he wants to use the military to solve other problems. And you

[227:44]wants to extricate itself from the Middle East and a long overdue from Europe, no longer

[227:49]seeing Russia as a strategic threat and focusing on Asia because that is really the future.

[253:11]to come here and ask for more. You think that I'm going to be forced to do anything with

[253:16]you, that I'm going to be forced into a deal and have to, and here I am in a working relationship.

[253:22]You don't know me.

[253:24]I'll end the whole relationship. I'll make you look like a bitch. I will humiliate you.

[253:29]I will embarrass you. I will send you home with nothing. I will send you home and make

[253:34]your people think it was your fault. And you want the Europeans to fund your defense? We'll

[253:40]let them. We'll see how much money they come up with. And maybe that was the, maybe that

[253:45]was the thinking behind the scenes. I'd like to think I,

[253:49]You can kind of get in Trump's head and understand how he thinks when it comes to this stuff.

[253:53]And I believe that rather than a truly emotional outburst, I think it was a continuation of

[254:03]the diplomacy that has been happening behind the scenes.

[254:06]Again, if you follow the whole story for weeks, it is resolving this question.

[254:15]Zelensky doesn't want to give up the fight. And even if the deal is made, Zelensky wants

[254:19]guarantees that it's not going to continue at a later date, but that can't be part of

[254:24]the deal. And the United States wants to make a deal. So there, you're not getting guarantees.

[254:33]That is at the core of it. And that was also at the core of the mineral deal. And if Zelensky

[254:41]And if Zelensky thinks he's in a position to negotiate, that's also a big problem.

[255:01]is not a part of the negotiation.

[255:03]He is a proxy.

[255:05]We are in charge.

[255:21]that they can't cash.

[255:23]They're writing checks that they expect us to cash.

[276:48]That is the sort of major fundamental realignment that's taking place.

[276:56]And it's simple, Russia has had to rely on China to spare themselves from the worst effects

[277:06]of the sanctions.

[277:18]The way that Russia is able to transport goods and resources in and out of Siberia and its rivers and in the Arctic Ocean is with the northern sea route.

[277:28]Well, because of sanctions, China has been doing all of the shipping up there in the Arctic.

[277:34]China is building ice-breaking ships.

[277:41]They've got a fleet of them.

[277:42]China is now making them.

[277:56]China, Russia and China, or rather, Russia in the United States said, China isn't in

[278:00]the Arctic.

[279:01]to own it.

[279:02]Russia doesn't want that either.

[279:05]So there's many different areas of competition where Russia and China are set against each

[279:14]in raw materials, in the Arctic, in Central Asia. And Russia is concerned about that,

[279:23]because in a relationship with China and Russia, Russia will now be the junior partner. It was

[279:29]reversed. Years ago, the Communist Chinese were a junior partner to the Soviet Union. Now that's

[279:46]like they always should have been.

[279:54]because the United States needs them.

[280:09]because Russia shares a border with China, not necessarily with the United States.

[280:14]So, that is the big picture. And this puts Moscow in a good position because whether

[280:19]they do the deal or not, whether that alignment occurs, you have Beijing and Washington competing

[280:24]for Moscow.

[280:27]And so Russia, and this is, this is the kind of thinking which characterizes multi polarity.

[280:35]So during the bipolar world order, you have the Soviet union in the United States and

[280:40]you either fell into the first world, second world or the third world, you're either with

[280:44]the capitalist West, with the communist East, or you were unaligned in the middle and you

[280:49]were a battleground or they were competing for your allegiance.

[280:55]world order after the fall of the Soviet Union. It was just the United States. Nobody could

[281:00]challenge the United States. Nobody could stand up to them. No, nobody's economy. Nobody's

[281:05]military even came close. The United States dominated every region. No, there were really,

[281:11]there were no superpowers. There were really no regional powers. It was just us. Now.

[281:18]After this sort of transitional period where Russia has led the resistance against the

[281:22]unipolar order, Russia has stood up to the United States.
